question: Where can the infer keyword be used?

options.text: Only in the true branch of a conditional type (after extends ... ?)
Anywhere in a type definition
options.explanation: infer is special syntax that only works inside conditional types in the extends pattern

options.text: In interfaces and classes
options.explanation: infer cannot be used in interfaces or classes — only in conditional types

options.text: In both branches of a conditional type (true and false)
options.explanation: A variable declared with infer is only available in the true branch of the conditional type

question: What happens when two infer with the same name appear in covariant positions?

options.text: A union of the inferred types is produced
An intersection of the inferred types is produced
options.explanation: Intersection occurs in contravariant positions (e.g., function parameters). In covariant positions, TypeScript creates a union

options.text: TypeScript throws a compilation error
options.explanation: Two infer with the same name is valid syntax — TypeScript will combine the inferred types

options.text: The second infer is ignored
options.explanation: Both infer declarations are considered — the result depends on position (covariant/contravariant)

question: What type results from: type R = UnwrapPromise<string>, given UnwrapPromise<T> = T extends Promise<infer U> ? U : T?

options.text: string
never
options.explanation: never is only returned if the false branch returns never. Here the false branch returns T, which is string

options.text: unknown
options.explanation: string is not a Promise, so the false branch fires, returning T (which is string)

options.text: Promise<string>
options.explanation: string doesn't match the Promise<infer U> pattern, so the original T = string is returned

question: How do you extract the last parameter type of a function using infer?

options.text: T extends (...args: [...infer _, infer L]) => unknown ? L : never
T extends (...args: infer P) => unknown ? P[P.length - 1] : never
options.explanation: You cannot use arithmetic like P.length - 1 at the type level. You need the variadic tuples pattern

options.text: T extends (last: infer L) => unknown ? L : never
options.explanation: This pattern only works for single-parameter functions, not for any number of parameters

options.text: T extends (...args: infer _, last: infer L) => unknown ? L : never
options.explanation: Rest parameter syntax doesn't allow splitting arguments this way. You need the [...infer _, infer L] pattern inside a tuple

question: What does constrained infer in TypeScript 4.7+ do?

options.text: Allows adding an extends constraint directly in the infer position
Simplifies code by replacing nested conditional types
Automatically narrows the type in the true branch to the specified constraint
Only works with primitive types (string, number, boolean)
options.explanation: Constrained infer works with any types, not just primitives — you can constrain with interfaces, classes, unions, etc.

options.text: Replaces the need for conditional types
options.explanation: Constrained infer is still used inside conditional types — it doesn't replace them, it simplifies them
